entry.title: New POD 2.0 Framework: Certificate Check
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series on SAP Digital Manufacturing new POD 2.0 framework, the new generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing worker UIs.

New POD 2.0 Framework Blogs

  1. The New Generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing Worker UIs
  2. Template-Based Design Instead of POD Types
  3. Visual WYSIWYG Designer
  4. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 1
  5. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 2
  6. Custom UI Dialogs and Calling Production Processes
  7. Dynamic PODs, How to Dynamically Show, Hide, Enable, and Disable UI Elements
  8. Other Business Capabilities Enabled by POD 2.0
  9. Interactive Value Journey (IVJ)
  10. POD Notifications
  11. Tips and Tricks
  12. Accessing PODs 2.0
  13. Implementing Data Scan for Barcode-enabled Component Consumption
  14. Electronic Batch Records (EBR)
  15. Certificate Check in POD 2.0

Introduction

In manufacturing environments, ensuring that only properly qualified personnel perform specific production activities is critical for maintaining product quality, regulatory compliance, operational safety, and workforce accountability. Organizations often need to verify that operators possess the required certifications before allowing them to execute tasks on the shop floor, particularly for specialized equipment, regulated processes, or safety-sensitive operations.

SAP Digital Manufacturing provides built-in Certification Management capabilities that can be associated with manufacturing objects such as Operations, Work Centers, and Resources. Combined with the flexibility of POD 2.0 and Production Processes, these capabilities can be used to automatically validate worker qualifications during production execution and prevent unauthorized activities from being started.

In this blog post, we will explore how to implement a certification validation mechanism in SAP Digital Manufacturing using a Production Process and POD 2.0 actions. By the end, you will have a configurable solution that checks operator certifications before a phase is started and displays a meaningful error message whenever the required qualifications are not assigned to the worker.

Setup

First, create new Certifications in Manage Certifications app, and assign to the Manufacturing Objects like an Operation Activity, Work Center and/or Resource, etc, where these Certifications are required to be assigned to individual workers, before they can start any manufacturing activities.

For this example, I create the two Certifications below:

Next, assign Certifications to Workers via Manage User Assignments app. In this example, I assigned only one of the Certifications to my own user.

Then, create a Production Process to perform the Certification Check using Production Process Design app.

The Production Process should have Input Parameters including: inMaterial, inOperation, inPlant, inResource, inUser and inWorkCenter, all of type String and marked as required.

On the left hand side, under ‘Services and Processes’, drag and drop ‘Certification Check’ service to the design canvas and map the INPUT parameters as follow:

Add a Condition control, and define (1) as 'Service_Certification_Check#isCertificationForbidden' == true:

Click '...' on the top-right corner, and select 'Manage Error Codes':

And create an Error Code like:

Add an Error End control,  select the newly created Error Code, and link the Condition output (1) to it. Link the Else (2) to the End control.

Finally, click "..." for the Cloud Process, select 'Edit Header', and enable 'Publish to Service Registry', so this Cloud Process can be triggered from the POD 2.0.

Save and deploy it.

Next, open Manage PODs 2.0 app and open / create a POD. In this example, I´m using an Order POD.

Select the PhaseList plugin and click on 'Edit Actions' under 'Start Phase' on the right-hand side Properties panel:

Click '+' to add a new Action, select 'ProductionProcess', map the newly created Production Process, link the Input Parameters to Variables from POD Context and make sure you use the Arrow Up button to move the Production Process up, before the StartPhase action, so it runs before the phase start and can prevent it in case of failure:

With all this setup in place, you should be ready to test it.

Run

To give it a try, click Run (Play button) in Manage PODs 2.0 app to load the POD, and then filter, select and open a order where the master data matches the assignment of the Certifications created in Manage Certifications app, and click 'Start'.

If the User has the Certification assigned, it should work just fine and the phase should be started:

However, if the Certification is not assigned, the Production Process should fail, fall on the Error End, and display the Error Code message defined:

Then, as soon as the missing Certification is assigned to the User in Manage User Assignments app:

The Phase Start should work just fine as well:

Conclusion

By combining SAP Digital Manufacturing Certifications, Production Processes, and POD 2.0 actions, manufacturers can implement robust certification controls directly within production execution workflows. This approach ensures that only appropriately certified workers can perform specific manufacturing activities, helping organizations improve compliance, reduce operational risk, and maintain consistent process quality.

As demonstrated in this example, the Certification Check service can be seamlessly integrated into the phase start process, allowing qualification validation to occur automatically and in real time. When certification requirements are not met, execution can be prevented immediately with clear user feedback, while certified operators can proceed without additional manual verification steps.

This pattern can be extended to a wide range of business scenarios, including safety-critical operations, regulated industries, specialized equipment usage, and workforce qualification management.

Leveraging the extensibility of POD 2.0 and Production Processes enables organizations to build powerful governance mechanisms while maintaining a streamlined and user-friendly operator experience.

 

Do you like this post? Please let me know in the comments section what you think. Any feedback is highly appreciated.
Or, if you have any questions, please check SAP Community Q&A Area, or comment down below.

Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: Freight Order Execution Monitoring
entry.content:
Freight Order Execution Monitoring app gives KPI based view of Freight Orders, so they do not need to open each document manually. From the monitoring screen, users can drill down into detailed execution status and take action where needed.

The app helps Dispatcher users to:

  • Monitor Freight Orders by execution status
  • Identify delayed or incomplete Freight Orders
  • Compare planned and actual execution updates
  • Review missing arrival or departure events
  • Support smoother settlement and reporting

This app is very useful for daily transportation operations. If execution events are missed, it can create issues later in charge calculation, settlement and tracking carrier payment. By using this app regularly, teams can catch execution gaps early and keep the Freight Order process transparent.

Activity On Time Performance:


Carrier On Time Performance:

Conclusion
The Freight Order Execution Monitoring app gives better visibility and control over the Freight Order lifecycle. It helps operations teams focus on exceptions, improve follow up, and ensure execution updates are completed before settlement processes begin.

Reference:
https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/e16bfa31fde2409a8422e36844ffc143.html?utm_source=chatgpt.comhttps://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_ON-PREMISE/e3dc5400c1cc41d1bc0ae0e7fd9aa5a2/e16bfa31fde2409a8422e36844ffc143.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com

entry.title: Manyat Layout: Shifting Inventory Readiness Ahead of Wave Execution in SAP EWM
entry.content:
Manyat Layout: Shifting Inventory Readiness Ahead of Wave Execution in SAP EWM

 

Introduction

While studying SAP EWM Wave Management, I kept asking myself a simple operational question:

Why do we wait for Wave execution before preparing inventory?

In many warehouse environments, outbound demand is already visible before execution begins. Sales orders may already exist, shipping schedules may already be known, and expected inventory requirements may already be identified.

This raises an interesting question:

If tomorrow's outbound demand is already known today, can part of the inventory preparation begin before Wave execution starts?

This question became the foundation of what I call the Manyat Layout Concept.

The objective of this article is not to replace SAP EWM functionality. Instead, it explores whether physical inventory preparation can begin earlier when outbound demand is already known.

Standard SAP EWM Wave Process

In SAP EWM, Wave Management is configured through standard Wave Management functionality.

Common transactions and areas associated with Wave processing include:

  • /SCWM/WAVE

  • /SCWM/WAVETMP

  • Wave Templates

  • Wave Release Processing

  • Warehouse Task Creation

  • Warehouse Order Creation Rules (WOCR)

A traditional outbound process often follows this sequence:

Demand → Wave Creation → Warehouse Tasks → WOCR → Warehouse Orders → Picking → Goods Issue

This process is proven and widely used across warehouse operations.

Most optimization activities begin after the Wave is released.

Warehouse Tasks are generated.

WOCR organizes the work.

Warehouse Orders are created.

Resources begin execution.

The Manyat Layout Concept

Manyat Layout introduces an additional preparation stage before execution:

Possible Preparation Before Standard Wave Execution

Demand → Material Identification → Replenishment → Readiness Zone

Standard SAP EWM Flow Continues

Wave Release → Warehouse Tasks → WOCR → Warehouse Orders → Picking → Goods Issue

The philosophy behind the concept is simple:

Prepare First. Execute Later.

The objective is not to replace SAP EWM functionality.

The objective is to explore whether inventory associated with expected outbound demand can be prepared before execution activities begin.

If inventory is already positioned closer to outbound operations, the warehouse may begin execution from a more prepared state.

What Is the Readiness Zone?

The Readiness Zone is not a traditional storage area.

It is not intended to become a buffer stock location.

Instead, it represents a preparation area where inventory associated with known outbound demand can be positioned before execution begins.

Only materials linked to expected demand are moved.

The concept remains demand-driven rather than storage-driven.

The objective is operational readiness rather than inventory accumulation.

What About WOCR?

A common question is:

If WOCR already organizes work, why is this concept needed?

WOCR remains an important SAP EWM functionality.

The concept does not replace WOCR.

The concept does not remove WOCR.

Manyat Layout does not ask how to replace WOCR.

It asks whether part of the preparation effort can happen before WOCR starts working.

If inventory is already positioned closer to outbound execution:

  • Picker travel distance may decrease.

  • Search activities may be reduced.

  • Warehouse preparation may improve.

  • Execution may become faster.

WOCR can still perform grouping and optimization activities according to demand and warehouse requirements.

The goal is not to replace SAP logic.

The goal is to reduce unnecessary movement before execution begins.

Another Perspective: Execution-Stage Dependencies

Another perspective involves execution-stage dependencies.

In many warehouse environments, execution activities begin only after multiple planning and grouping steps have been completed.

If an issue occurs during these stages, warehouse execution may be delayed while corrective actions are performed.

When inventory has already been prepared and positioned in a Readiness Zone, the warehouse enters execution from a more prepared state.

The concept does not eliminate system issues.

However, it raises an interesting operational question:

Can early inventory preparation reduce the operational impact of execution-stage disruptions by improving warehouse readiness before execution begins?

This article does not claim a definitive answer.

Instead, it explores whether preparation itself can become an additional layer of operational readiness before standard SAP EWM execution processes begin.

A Real-World Example

Consider a warehouse that already knows the next morning's outbound requirements.

Under a traditional approach, inventory remains in its original storage locations until execution begins.

Under the Manyat Layout approach:

  • Required inventory is identified early.

  • Replenishment moves inventory into a Readiness Zone.

  • Inventory is prepared before execution starts.

  • Wave processing and WOCR continue as standard SAP EWM activities.

When execution begins, the warehouse starts from a prepared state rather than a waiting state.

Where AI Can Help

Artificial Intelligence introduces additional possibilities.

AI can support this concept by:

  • Analyzing historical demand patterns.

  • Identifying fast-moving materials.

  • Predicting future outbound demand.

  • Supporting replenishment decisions.

  • Recommending Readiness Zone preparation.

As forecasting becomes more accurate, warehouse preparation may become increasingly proactive rather than reactive.

The Real Question

This article does not attempt to replace SAP EWM standard functionality.

Instead, it raises a practical operational question:

If outbound demand is already known, can inventory preparation begin before Wave execution starts?

And if preparation begins earlier:

Can warehouse readiness improve before execution activities even begin?

Conclusion

Traditional warehouse optimization often focuses on execution.

Manyat Layout focuses on preparation.

The concept suggests that some of the most valuable warehouse improvements may occur before the first picking activity begins.

Fast picking creates activity.

Prepared picking creates efficiency.

And sometimes the biggest warehouse optimization happens before the first pick starts.

— Rahul Kumar

Manyat Thinking

X-Raying Warehouse Operations Through SAP EWM

entry.title: Re: BO 4.3 SP05 upgrade failing because of SQL Anywhere logs
entry.content:
With the help of SAP Support, we discovered a very strange thing.

In the setupengine.log file, about 30 minutes after the start of the upgrade, there is this entry:

18:50:30.540 RunCommand: Get CmdLine: E:\SAPBusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\bin64\dbsvc -t Network -s Automatic -as -i -sn "SQL Anywhere for SAP Business Intelligence" -sd "Provides the CMS repository and auditing database for SAP Business Intelligence" -y -w "SQLAnywhereForBI" "E:\SAPBusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\bin64\dbsrv17.exe" -n "BI4" -x tcpip(PORT=2638;DoBroadcast=NO;BroadcastListener=NO) "E:\SAPBusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\database\BI4_CMS.db" "E:\SAPBusinessObjects\sqlanywhere\database\BI4_Audit.db"

This is strange and incorrect, since the database files used by this system are in another path, on disk G: !!!

This could be the cause of the error that causes the upgrade to fail, however, I wonder, why does the installer try to register the SQL Anywhere service by looking for the database files in an incorrect path?
Is there a way to fix it or have I to find a work around (i.e. a sym link ...)

Thank you all.

entry.title: Re: Stock Inconsistency in MC.9 Report
entry.content:
Hello @Ravi26 

You have tagged your question with "S/4HANA". If you are really on S/4HANA consider the following notes explaining Inventory Controlling including MC.9 situation in the S/4HANA context:

  • 2319579 - S4TWL - Performance optimizations within Material Document Processing - lock behavior and stock underrun protection
  • 2659269 - Error M5 017 - ''No data exists for chosen selection'' popped up when use Inventory controlling reports
  • 3126267 - What are successor Fiori apps for Inventory Controlling (Application 03) report like MC.9 in S/4HANA system?

Regarding MMBE vs MC.9 differences, see the note 3552049 - Mismatch between MC.9 and MMBE Report

As @Lakshmipathi explained MMBE and MC.9 pull data from different sources. Results of both reports are consistent in well maintained systems and they reconcile with FI - been there, done that. Still data sources are different, thus results might be different. 

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: OData vs SOAP for .NET WMS Integration in SAP Public...
entry.content: Hello Henrike, Thank you very much for your feedback and for the detailed information. The proposed approach is indeed functional in SAP Business ByDesign. Our customer is currently using the logistics task concept in ByD, where warehouse operators receive and process warehouse tasks generated by the system (goods receipt, stock transfer, picking, etc.) through our WMS application. The key question from the customer is how to achieve a similar process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. More specifically, we would like to understand how the warehouse task concept available in ByD can be leveraged or replicated in Public Cloud WM. Are warehouse tasks created and managed in Public Cloud in a way that can be consumed and processed by an external WMS through APIs, similar to the SiteLogisticsTask and WarehouseConfirmation services in ByD? If so, which APIs or business objects would be the equivalent entry points? Our objective is to provide the customer with the same operational experience they currently have in ByD while respecting the Public Cloud architecture and clean core principles. Thank you in advance for your guidance. Kind regards,

entry.title: Re: Material Description missing in Compare Supplier Quot...
entry.content:
Dear @khaing63,

In Public Cloud the material description cannot be enabled in the standard Compare Supplier Quotations app by using Adapt UI. This is a standard app limitation, not an authorization issue. SAP confirms for the Compare Supplier Quotations app that additional fields cannot be added. The app uses backend service MM_PUR_QTN_COMPARE_SRV and the CDS views C_RequestForQuotationCompare and C_SupplierQuotationCompare. These CDS views do not support metadata extension, therefore the app does not provide extensibility for adding further fields such as material description

The standard Compare Supplier Quotations app should therefore be used for the delivered quotation comparison and awarding process only. If the material description is required, it needs to be checked in the related RFQ or supplier quotation item details, for example via Manage RFQs / Manage Supplier Quotations, depending on the process used

If the business requires a side-by-side comparison including material description, the clean-core approach would be to create a separate custom analytical/list report or side-by-side extension based on released procurement data sources/APIs. The standard Compare Supplier Quotations app itself should not be positioned as extensible for this requirement. In parallel, if this requirement is important for the standard product, it can be submitted as an improvement request via SAP Influence where customers and partners can submit and vote on non-go-live-critical improvement requests

More details below:

  • SAP KBA 3411976 – Extend the app Compare Supplier Quotations
  • SAP Help Compare Supplier Quotations
  • SAP Influence

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Warehouse Task Concept in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition – Mapping from SAP Business ByDesign Inte
entry.content:
We are currently working with a customer who uses a .NET-based solution integrated with SAP Business ByDesign.

In the current ByD setup, warehouse operators work based on logistics tasks generated by the system (e.g., Goods Receipt, Stock Transfer, Picking). Our WMS consumes these tasks and allows operators to execute and confirm them. This process is supported through APIs such as SiteLogisticsTask and WarehouseConfirmation.

 

The customer is now evaluating a transition to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and would like to maintain the same operational model.

 

While we understand that S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides Warehouse Management capabilities and supports API-based integration with external systems, we are struggling to identify the equivalent of the ByD logistics task concept.

 

Our questions are:

  • Is there an equivalent to ByD Logistics Tasks / Warehouse Tasks in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
  • Can these tasks be created, retrieved, processed, and confirmed through standard APIs by an external WMS?
  • Which business objects and APIs should be considered as the equivalent of SiteLogisticsTask and WarehouseConfirmation in ByD?
  • Are there any limitations or recommended approaches when implementing a task-driven warehouse execution process with an external WMS in Public Cloud?

Our objective is to offer the customer the same warehouse execution experience they currently have in ByD while following SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition best practices and Clean Core principles.

Any guidance, architecture recommendations, or references to relevant APIs and business objects would be greatly appreciated.

entry.title: AI-Powered Customer Experience in the Travel and Transportation Industry
entry.content:
Delivering Seamless, Personalized Journeys

When we talk about Travel and Transportation there is only one thing a business should focus on, and that is ‘end-to-end’ customer journey. Excellence in experience is what you strive for from the very first point of interaction to the destination. Travelers nowadays have very strict requirements, from booking a ticket to arriving at a destination, they expect fast, convenient, and prompt assistance when they face issues along the way. No matter how perfectly we design a system to meet the needs, there will always be situations where we can’t avoid. Delays, confusing booking systems, long customer service wait times and many more creates frustration for travelers.

AI (Artificial Intelligence) is reshaping how Travel and Transportation industry is doing business. It delivers more avenues to provide customer care aside from the typical communication channels such as email, short messaging services, and social media. AI provides smarter, faster and more personalized customer experiences leading to happier customers which entails to growth in revenue for the business. AI is no longer optional, it is now becoming a necessity – The question is, HOW?

Beyond Tickets and Timetables: How AI Orchestrates the Customer Journey

Previously, travelers preferred travel agents over booking apps, relied on printed tickets, and valued personal service and human interaction. However, mobile apps for bookings, check-ins, and payments are now widely used. Travelers also expect real-time updates and personalized recommendations that provide seamless, end-to-end experiences.

SAP delivers an ecosystem that provides all the tools needed to meet these expectations. Using Service Cloud Version 2, organizations can manage cases, complaints, and information requests efficiently. AI can respond within seconds, unlike traditional customer service processes that rely on manual handling of support tickets. AI-powered chatbots can also manage a high volume of customer conversations simultaneously.

Here is an example integration strategy,

Data Type

Target Systems

Description

Customer Profile

Sales, Service, Commerce and Marketing

Implement for centralize account management, B2B/B2C Partnerships and real time account updates

Booking

Commerce, Service, and Marketing

Leverage AI driven product recommendations to automatically suggest hotel accommodations, flight and transportation combinations

Browsing Behavior

Commerce, Sales, and Marketing

Create dynamic customer segments based on travel preferences, booking patterns and engagement history

Loyalty Status

SAP Loyalty Management Program, Commerce

A centralized loyalty platform enables businesses to manage diverse loyalty programs, rewards, tiers, and promotions across multiple brands and regions from a single system while maintaining local flexibility and control

Support Interactions

Service

Provides unified customer support across chat, email and social media

AI

Sales, Service, and Commerce

Leverage AI to provide contextual travel recommendations based on customer preferences

Commerce Cloud Intelligent Selling Services

Intelligent Selling Services (ISS) for SAP Commerce Cloud is an AI-powered enhancement that leverages machine learning and artificial intelligence to deliver personalized customer experiences and optimize booking strategies. This help in analyzing customer booking pattern and provide contextual data across customer touchpoint and can provide recommendations which can lead increase in revenue.

Commerce Cloud Travel Accelerator

This industry-specific solution is designed to help travel companies deliver omnichannel digital traveler engagement through SAP Commerce Cloud. Organizations that already have an existing SAP Commerce Cloud solution can leverage this add-on to tailor the platform to their unique business requirements. By utilizing this solution, companies can access real-time information to deliver personalized customer experiences, strengthen customer loyalty, and drive greater business value.

Loyalty Management Program through Integration

Of course, a customer will need a system to reward customers for coming back. it’s like earning points, perks, or special treatment when you repeatedly book with the same travel company, for that an integration to loyalty management program either our very own solution or a 3rd party solution can be used.

The Way Forward

With the Advanced Success Plan, we can help you achieve your business goals. As a starting point, we will create a service engagement plan that provides a tailored approach to meeting your KPIs. During this phase, we will also deliver sessions to help you set up Sales Cloud, Service Cloud, and Commerce Cloud while ensuring that Travel and Transportation industry best practices are followed.

With AI capabilities available across every solution, you can now categorize your customer base based on travel behaviors and patterns, as well as perform sentiment analysis on customer reviews and support tickets. The SAP Advanced Success Plan can serve as a strategic partner in helping you achieve your AI objectives. Our experts, backed by deep industry knowledge, can provide guidance on the most effective path forward.

To deliver services that are aligned with each customer's specific goals, we have organized our offerings into four phases: implementation, pre-go-live, post-go-live, and continuous improvement.

Implementation:

During the implementation phase, our focus is on providing adoption guidance to help you set up your solutions, from front-end applications to back-end systems. We work alongside your team to establish the core capabilities needed for a successful implementation and to ensure the solution is aligned with your business requirements.

This includes, but is not limited to, application user management, key user extensibility, the CX AI Toolkit, integrations, security considerations, and other essential platform capabilities. Our goal is to help you build a solid foundation that supports scalability, maintainability, and future growth while enabling your teams to get the most value from the platform.

Pre-Go Live:

In the pre-go-live phase, our focus is to validate and safeguard the solutions that have been built throughout the implementation. The goal is to make sure your systems are configured correctly, performing as expected, and ready when you go live.

This includes conducting detailed reviews of business configuration settings, evaluating system performance, validating integrations, reviewing security and user access setups, and assessing analytics and reporting capabilities. We also help identify potential risks, gaps, or areas for optimization before launch, ensuring that any issues are addressed proactively rather than after go-live.

In addition, we work with your teams to confirm readiness across key functional and technical areas, helping ensure that testing has been completed successfully, critical business scenarios have been validated, and the solution is aligned with your operational requirements. By performing an Adoption Checkpoint during this phase, we help reduce risk, improve system stability, and support a smoother go-live experience.

Post-Go Live:

During the post-go-live phase, we work closely with your team to ensure that recommendations and best practices identified throughout the implementation have been properly configured and are delivering the intended results.

As your users begin working in the production environment, new questions, opportunities for optimization, and minor challenges often emerge. During this stage, we provide continued guidance and support to help address those items, whether they are related to business processes, system configuration, integrations, extensibility, analytics, or overall solution adoption.

Our functional and technical experts remain available to review issues, provide recommendations, and help your team navigate any areas that require additional attention. We also help identify opportunities for further improvements and knowledge transfer, ensuring your organization is well-positioned to maintain, enhance, and scale the solution over time.

Continuous Improvement:

Finally, as part of the continuous improvement phase, we help ensure that you remain informed about new innovations and enhancements introduced through SAP release cycles. By staying up to date with the latest capabilities, you can continue to maximize the value of your solutions and drive ongoing business success.

entry.title: WMS API Equivalents in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition vs. SAP Business ByDesign
entry.content:
We are currently working with a customer who uses a .NET-based solution integrated with SAP Business ByDesign.

In the current ByD setup, warehouse operators work based on logistics tasks generated by the system (e.g., Goods Receipt, Stock Transfer, Picking). Our WMS consumes these tasks and allows operators to execute and confirm them. This process is supported through APIs such as SiteLogisticsTask and WarehouseConfirmation.

The customer is now evaluating a transition to SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition and would like to maintain the same operational model.

 

While we understand that S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition provides Warehouse Management capabilities and supports API-based integration with external systems, we are struggling to identify the equivalent of the ByD logistics task concept.

 

Our questions are:

  • Is there an equivalent to ByD Logistics Tasks / Warehouse Tasks in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
  • Can these tasks be created, retrieved, processed, and confirmed through standard APIs by an external WMS?

entry.title: The living loop: Circular Manufacturing Framework with Nesting Intelligence - Assisted by AI
entry.content:
The core problem

Conventional scrap reduction fails because it treats symptoms, not the system. Three structural blind spots drive most losses: 3PL warehouses operating without real-time visibility or remnant data; nesting optimized per job rather than across the full material lifecycle; and SAP's Material Ledger doesn't the full information, leaving variances unattributed and finance unable to trace losses to root causes.

The achieved result - Citing a business use case.

At a major aerospace OEM, the Manufacturing Loop reduced titanium scrap from 26% to 21% across 13,000 production orders and $900M of managed inventory, delivering a verified $5M bottom-line saving within 12 months.

  

Annual titanium spends

$448M

Scrap pre-program

$116.5M ($448M × 26%)

Scrap post-program

$94.1M ($448M × 21%)

Gross recovery potential

$22.4M

Verified net saving (Phase 1)

$5M (after ~$17.4M program costs)

The four implementation levers

  1. Nesting optimization — Cross-order, cross-inventory nesting that queries SAP in real time, prioritizes remnants, and flags additive manufacturing where it is the lower-cost route. The primary driver of the 5-point reduction.
  2. 3PL integration — Connected $900M of previously invisible inventory to SAP procurement logic, stopping duplicate purchasing against stock already paid for, including taking effect of the remnants in stock. 
  3. Additive manufacturing — Introduced for highest-scrap geometries as a material cost decision. Buy-to-fly ratio: 8:1–16:1 conventional vs 1.5:1–2:1 additive.
  4. SAP Material Ledger & product costing — Activated actual costing with full information, to create attributable variance lines (scrap, yield, purchase price, production), making the $5M visible and defensible.

Phased path to 60–70% scrap reduction

Phase

Scrap

Saving

Basis

Phase 1 — Achieved

26% → 21%

$5M

Verified

Phase 2 — Projected

21% → 14–15%

~$10M

Additive at scale + remnant automation

Phase 3 — Projected

14–15% → 8–10%

~$8M

Closed-loop governance + SAP actual costing

Cumulative

26% → 8–10%

~$23M

62–69% reduction — within 60–70% range

    

The framework: four layers

  • Diagnose — Waste mapping, root cause analysis, value stream mapping
  • Activate — Nesting engine drawing from live SAP inventory before triggering procurement
  • Build — Dual sourcing, connected 3PL, predictive replenishment
  • Sustain — SAP Material Ledger delivering actual cost visibility across all variance categories

Governance

Daily OEE reviews · Weekly scrap reviews · Monthly Material Ledger variance analysis · Quarterly 3PL and supplier audits · Statistical process control · Digital part traceability back to sheet, nest, and production order.

The return

Outcome

Figure

Basis

Scrap rate reduction

60–70%

Phase 1 verified at 19.2%; full program 62–69% cumulative

OEE improvement

10–15 pts

Aerospace baseline 55–70%; 5–10 pts per phase 

Annual recoverable value

$10–20M

Modelled from $448M spend at program maturity; Phase 1 verified at $5M

The Manufacturing Loop underlines the fact that scrap is not an unavoidable cost of precision manufacturing — it is a diagnostic signal pointing directly to isolated systems, unscored remnants, and financial blind spots that have persisted simply because no one has built the bridge between the nesting engine and the SAP Material Ledger. By connecting those two worlds in real time, manufacturers managing high-value materials like titanium can recover millions in previously invisible losses — as verified through a $5M saving achieved at a major Tier 1 aerospace OEM within 12 months.

If you are working in a manufacturing environment where scrap variance is visible on your P&L but untraceable to a root cause, start with one question: does your nesting engine know what SAP knows right now? The answer will tell you exactly where your loop is broken — and where to close it first.

I would love to hear from experts and practitioners in the field — whether you have faced similar integration challenges, implemented parts of this framework, or had questions about how the SAP Material Ledger fits into your specific environment. Please share your thoughts, experiences, or questions in the comments below. Every conversation makes the loop a little tighter.

entry.title: Re: Cloud Connector 2.19.0.2 is here!
entry.content:
Hi Devin,

the fact that Tomcat has a security vulnerability does not automatically mean, that a Cloud Connector installation is affected by this. Cloud Connector has embedded Tomcat libraries adapted to its needs and only uses a small portion of Tomcat features. Furthermore, Cloud Connector is not meant to act as a public web server, which means that even if a Tomcat vulnerability would be relevant, then this usually would only be exploitable from within the own intranet, but not from cloud side or from the internet.

I can tell you that security issues of all used Open Source components - which of course also includes Tomcat - are actively monitored and assessed by responsible SAP teams, and appropriate security patches will be published asap, if required.

However, what is also part of the security principles, is that we do not discuss security vulnerabilities or disclose any related info in a public forum like this. So, if you have security concerns, and would like to discuss them, please create an SAP support case for this.
Thank you for your understanding.

entry.title: New Course Extending SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 using In-App Extensibility
entry.content:
Walking towards the summer (or winter depending where you are) with you SAP CX Community! Fernando is here, checking in and kicking off a new SAP CX Integration and Extensibility blog post.

From last year onwards, our learning production focus is on SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2 extensibility and integration topics. Today, I'm delighted to announce in this blog post the next major learning milestone for those looking to master the InApp extensibility of SAP Sales and Service Cloud version 2.

So, please, join me in welcoming our newest addition to the SAP's learning platform: 


New Course Extending SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 using In-App Extensibility

If you're an adminsitrator,  business consultant, architect, or developer looking to customize and enhance SAP Sales and Service Cloud version 2 using built‑in tools, you've come to the right place. Here, you'll gain the practical knowledge needed to implement your custom business requirements.

We've structured our learning content to ensure that, after finishing this course participants will have the skills to create and modify custom fields, implement business rules, adapt UI elements and interact with external systems within SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2.

Here's a glimpse of what it looks like:

 


Audience
The course is meant to be helpful for:

  • Administrators
  • Business Users
  • Developers
  • Technical Consultants
  • Architects

Learning Objectives
This course teaches how to customize and enhance SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2 using built‑in tools without deep programming.. You’ll follow a practical knowledge covering personalization and adaptation of the UI, extending business entities, implementing custom logic,  and to interact with external systems.

Learning Content
The course consists of 4 units and provides more than 2 hours of content:

  • Introducing In-App Extensibility in SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud Version 2
    Presents In‑app extensibility in SAP Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 to enable businesses to quickly and safely tailor applications through intuitive UI tools delivering flexible, upgrade‑proof enhancements with minimal effort.
  •  Enhancing User Experience with Personalization and Adaptation
    Fine tune user experience in SAP Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 through personalization and adaptation capabilities that let users tailor interfaces, configure behavior, and assign dynamic page layouts to match individual roles and business needs.
  • Extending Business Entities
    Customizing business entities in SAP Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 empowers organizations to enrich standard objects by adding custom fields, implementing tailored business logic, and managing dynamic property rules to support more flexible and intelligent business processes.
  • Interacting with External Systems
    The integration of external systems in SAP Sales Cloud and Service Cloud V2 enables seamless integration through interaction types, external hooks, mashups with web applications, and automated workflows, helping streamline processes and extend functionality beyond the core system.

After completing successfully this course you'll be granted with a digital badge like this one which could be shared and linked to your social networks:

Enroll on  it today!  We’re launching an exciting new series of extensibility learning content for SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2, join us to boost your team’s skills and share feedback to help shape even better lessons.

Additional Resources
You can find a related live session on In-App extensibility for SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud version 2. For additional free learning journeys and courses, visit the SAP Learning site for Integration and Extesibility to build in-demand SAP skills on a variety of topics, for the SAP CX Solutions portfolio. If you enjoy quick video learning, explore our Integration and Extensibility videos for SAP Customer Experience for the newest updates on integration and extensibility for SAP Customer Experience solutions.

entry.title: Unable to Reconcile Vendor Down Payment with AP Invoice Containing Withholding Tax in SAP Business O
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I am facing an issue in SAP Business One 10.0 while trying to reconcile a Vendor Down Payment against an AP Invoice.

Scenario:

  1. An AP Invoice was created approximately one month ago.

  2. Later, a Vendor Down Payment was made to the same vendor.

  3. The vendor has adjusted the down payment amount against the AP Invoice.

  4. I am now attempting to reconcile the Vendor Down Payment with the AP Invoice through Internal Reconciliation.

Document Details:

  • AP Invoice Amount: INR 2,915,439.50

  • Withholding Tax (WHT): INR 2,500.00

  • Vendor Down Payment: INR 11,141,570.00

Issue:
When attempting the reconciliation, SAP Business One displays the following error message:

"You can only link to the section of the document which has no withholding tax."

I would like to understand:

  • Is this a standard SAP Business One limitation when an AP Invoice contains Withholding Tax?

  • What is the recommended approach to reconcile a Vendor Down Payment against an AP Invoice that includes WHT?

  • Are there any additional steps or workarounds required to complete the reconciliation?

Any guidance would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: Where can I see SXI_MONITOR errors after successful r...
entry.content:
Hi,

The Only Places You May Still Find It
If the event was recent enough, check OS-level traces:

ECC ABAP Instance

Work process traces in:

  • ST11
  • dev traces:
    • dev_w*
    • dev_rfc*
    • dev_icm
    • dev_rd

Look around the exact timestamp.

Typical transient entries:

  • partner not reached
  • HTTPIO_PLG_CANCELED
  • connection reset
  • timeout during receive
  • location CPIC
  • ICM_HTTP_CONNECTION_BROKEN

 

PI/PO Java Side

If using Java Adapter Engine:

In:

  • NWA logs
  • defaultTrace
  • XPI Inspector traces

You may still find:

  • adapter reconnects
  • HTTP 503
  • JMS reconnects
  • temporary channel failures

Rgds

Dino

For the full list, the best source is SAP’s own documentation. All S/4HANA Utilities scope items are published in SAP Process Navigator, and this is the most accurate place to check so nothing is missed.

You can browse the complete list here:

https://me.sap.com/processnavigator 

SAP Process Navigator – S/4HANA Utilities (Public Cloud & Private Cloud):

Just filter by: Industry → Utilities and Solution → SAP S/4HANA

This will show every official scope item (Billing, Device Management, Move‑In/Out, Meter Reading, FI‑CA, etc.) with the latest updates.

This way you can validate your LeanIX model directly against SAP’s published content.

entry.title: Why is deployment mandatory for a root item type if the documentation says GenericItems is used as a
entry.content:
I'm reading the SAP Commerce documentation regarding item type deployments, which states:

Failing to declare a deployment for a type means that all instances of that type are stored in the genericitems table with all the other types that have no deployment table...

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_COMMERCE_CLOUD_PUBLIC_CLOUD/51e73d14aedc487384e4518b60a1f5fd/8ecae959b9bd46b8b426fa8dbde5cac4.html#:~:text=Failing%20to%20declare%20a%20deployment%20for%20a%20type%20means%20that%20all%20instances%20of%20that%20type%20are%20stored%20in%20the%20genericitems%20table%20with%20all%20the%20other%20types%20that%20have%20no%20deployment%20table. 

Based on this, my understanding was that if a type does not define a deployment, SAP Commerce would fall back to storing instances in the genericitems table, although this is discouraged due to performance implications.

My code

 
<itemtype code="MyCustomType"
          autocreate="true"
          generate="true"
          extends="GenericItem">
    <attributes>
        <attribute qualifier="name" type="java.lang.String">
            <persistence type="property"/>
        </attribute>
    </attributes>
</itemtype>

However, the build fails during validation with the following error:

[ycheckdeployments] No deployment defined for non-abstract itemType MyCustomType in file: .../resources/myextension-items.xml

Could someone clarify how these two behaviors fit together?

  • Is the GenericItems fallback described in the documentation referring to older platform behavior?

Thanks

entry.title: SAP SUM Reset: When, Why, and How to Reset SUM
entry.content:
Hello Reader,

Let's be real — if you're reading this, things probably haven't gone according to plan. Your SUM run is stuck somewhere in the Execution phase, your client wants answers, and the clock is ticking.
You're not alone. This is one of the most common and stressful situations a BASIS administrator can face.

This guide will walk you through everything you need to know — not just the commands, but the logic behind them — so you can make confident decisions under pressure.

Moreover, this would be a Quick Answers to What You're Probably Googling Right Now:

  • Can I reset SUM right now?
  • How to reset the SUM?
  • What happens if I just restore the database?
  • What if I've already deleted the SUM directory?

What Does a SUM Reset Actually Do?

A SUM reset is designed to bring the system back to a state where the upgrade can be restarted.

Depending on the phase and scenario, SUM can:

  • Reverse upgrade activities
  • Remove upgrade artifacts
  • Clean up the shadow instance
  • Restore upgrade consistency

A reset is different from:

  • Stopping SUM
  • Deleting the SUM directory
  • Restoring only the database

When Should You Consider a SUM Reset?

A SUM reset is typically considered in the following situations:

  1. Upgrade Stuck in Execution Phase
    You have spent considerable time analysing logs and retrying phases, but the upgrade is still blocked.
  2. Downtime Window Is Running Out
    Business requirements may force you to abandon the upgrade and return the system to service.
  3. Upgrade State Has Become Inconsistent
    Repeated failures, corrupted upgrade metadata, or unexpected SUM behaviour may require a complete restart.
  4. Customer Requests Rollback
    Sometimes the safest option is to stop the upgrade and plan another attempt later.

When Is a Reset Actually Possible?

This is one of the most important things to understand before you do anything. SUM's ability to reset depends on two conditions:

Condition

Can You Reset?

Notes

Cleanup has NOT been run

Yes

This is the normal rollback path

Cleanup HAS been run (after success)

No

You must use a pre-upgrade system backup

You chose Reset, reset completed

Cleanup is now mandatory

You must run cleanup after a successful reset

SUM directory was deleted manually

Partially

Re-extract SAR file, then follow the stuck-phase procedure

Important: Never run the Cleanup option from SUM's More menu unless you are 100% sure you won't need to roll back. Once cleanup runs, the only way back is a full system restore from a backup taken before the upgrade began.

 

Why Restoring the Database Is Not Enough

One of the most overlooked aspects of SAP upgrades is the shadow instance.

During an upgrade, SUM creates a shadow system that consumes:

  • CPU
  • Memory
  • Disk
  • SAP resources

If you only restore the database:

✓ Database returns to its previous state

X Shadow instance may still exist

X Upgrade artifacts may remain

X Future upgrades may encounter issues

This is why a proper SUM reset is often necessary after restoring the database.

 

Best Practice Before Entering Execution Phase

Before any major upgrade activity begins, make sure you have:

Full Database Backup

This is your primary recovery mechanism.

Backup of the SUM Directory

I strongly recommend taking a backup of the complete SUM directory at the end of preprocessing.

If a rollback becomes necessary later, this backup can save a significant amount of time.

 

How to Reset SUM Correctly

Scenario 1: SUM Reset Option Is Available

This is the easiest situation.

From the SUM UI: More → Reset

Allow SUM to complete the reset process.

After completion:

  • Upgrade changes are reversed
  • Shadow instance is removed
  • Upgrade state is cleaned up

Scenario 2: Upgrade Is Already Deep Into Execution

If rollback is required:

Step 1: Restore and recover the database.

Step 2: Restore the backed-up SUM directory.

Step 3: Start SUM using the restored backup.

Step 4: Execute the reset.

This ensures both the database and SUM state are returned to a consistent condition.

Manual Reset Procedure

In certain situations, you may need to perform a manual reset preparation.

Navigate to:

SUM/abap/bin

Execute:

SAPup reset prepare

Afterward:

  1. Stop all SAPup processes at OS level.
  2. Delete the SUM directory.
  3. Extract a fresh SUM package if needed.
  4. Start again from the beginning.

Common Mistake #1: Deleting the SUM Directory

Many administrators believe that deleting the SUM folder automatically resets the upgrade.

It does not.

SUM stores upgrade information inside the SAP system database.

When a new SUM package is extracted, it often reads the existing upgrade state and returns to the same phase.

Always perform a proper reset procedure rather than simply deleting SUM.

Common Mistake #2: Running Cleanup Too Early

After an update, SUM provides:

More → Cleanup

Be careful.

Once Cleanup has been executed:

  • Reset is no longer available
  • Rollback capability is removed

At that point, database restoration may be the only recovery option.

 

What If SUM Keeps Dropping Back into the Same Phase?

This happens when the Execution phase ran far enough to make changes to the system's upgrade versioning tables (UVERS), but not far enough to complete cleanly. Even after you clear the SUM directory and re-extract the SAR file, SUM reads those database tables and drops straight back into the last active phase.

This is not a bug. SUM is doing exactly what it's designed to do — resuming where it left off. The problem is that the state it's resuming from is inconsistent.

Ex: SAP Note 1790486 covers the scenario where SAP_ABA (or another software component) is in an undefined upgrade state that is not safe to proceed with.

In emergency situations, SAP may recommend running report RSUPGRES:

Important considerations:

  • Execute with DDIC
  • Use client 000
  • Run in background
  • Do not cancel the job manually

The report is intended to repair upgrade status inconsistencies and should be used carefully.

Note: RSUPGRES is a powerful and potentially destructive tool. Use it only as a last resort and follow every instruction. Do not improvise.

Afterward, many administrators also execute:

/$SYNC

and

/$TAB

to refresh buffers and synchronize system status.

 

A Word on Cleanup — Don't Confuse It with Reset

SUM has two end-of-process options that BASIS admins sometimes confuse:

Option

When It Appears

What It Does

Can You Reset After?

Reset

During the update process

Rolls back the upgrade, dissolves the shadow instance

Yes — but cleanup becomes mandatory afterward

Cleanup

After a successful update OR after a Reset

Removes SUM working files and state

No — reset is no longer possible after this

Note: Cleanup is a one-way door. Once you walk through it, you cannot come back without a full system restore. Always think twice before clicking it.

 

Key Takeaways

Most BASIS admins who've gone through a reset once develop a pre-Execution checklist they follow religiously. Here's what that looks like:

Before every upgrade:

✓ Take a full database backup

✓ Take a backup of the SUM directory

✓ Understand your rollback strategy

When an upgrade fails:

✓ Determine whether a SUM reset is still possible

✓ Do not immediately delete the SUM directory

✓ Consider the shadow instance impact

✓ Restore both the database and SUM state when necessary

Always remember that a successful rollback is not just about restoring the database. A complete recovery requires restoring upgrade consistency and ensuring the shadow instance is properly removed.

 

Have you run into a reset scenario that isn't covered here?

Drop it in the comments.

entry.title: Einvoicing Poland KSEF issue with chain transaction with shipping point = DE
entry.content:
Hi SAP experts,

we have an issue with SAP DRC for Poland / KSEF. We have implemented e-invoicing for our Polish location.

We are currently experiencing an issue with invoices that are not being registered for KSeF and I would like to know if any of you have the same issue?

The issue comes up, when an order is placed within the Polish company code, but production and shipping take place in Germany (shipping point = Germany), while the invoice is issued from Poland. Tax settings are correct, and the invoice destination country in the billing invoice is also set to Poland.

According to our Polish accounting department, this case must be reported to KSeF because it constitutes a chain transaction due to the internal billing involved. However, SAP does not recognize it as KSeF-relevant—since the shipping point is not in Poland—so it does not appear in the eDocument Cockpit. There is a sap note that suggests to change the shipping point, but from our point of view this is not the correct way to handle it, because it will mess up reporting and intrastat.

We've opened an SAP incident but so far got no help. Has anyone else encountered this issue, and if so, how did you resolve it?

Thanks in advance

#shippingpoint #ksef #chain transaction

entry.title: Top 10 Best Practices for SAP Sustainability Control Tower
entry.content:
Top 10 Best Practices for SAP Sustainability Control Tower

  1. Establish Cross-Functional Governance: Form a steering committee including Sustainability, Finance, and IT. Define clear data ownership across business units before configuring the software.

  2. Adopt the "Finance-First" Mindset: Treat your sustainability data exactly like financial data. Implement strict internal controls and validation checks to ensure audit-ready ESG reporting.

  3. Automate ERP Integration: Eliminate manual data uploads via files. Use standard APIs to connect SCT directly with SAP S/4HANA and SAP SuccessFactors for real-time visibility.

  4. Leverage Supplier Data Exchange: For Scope 3 emissions, avoid generic industry averages. Use SAP SDX to receive actual product footprint data from supply chain partners.

  5. Master Compliance Frameworks Early: Pre-align your existing corporate data models with built-in SCT content for major frameworks like CSRD and EU Taxonomy.

  6. Stick to Clean Core Strategy: Keep your integration architecture clean. Avoid custom middleware inside SCT and utilize SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) if extensions are needed.

  7. Start with a Focused Scope: Adopt a phased approach. Begin with high-priority, readily available metrics (like Scope 1 and 2) before scaling to complex Scope 3 KPIs.

  8. Define Validation Protocols: Establish clear rules for handling messy or anomalous data. Know exactly who is responsible for fixing source data errors before year-end audits.

  9. Utilize Performance Forecasting: Don't just look back. Use built-in target setting and "What-if" simulations to model sustainability scenarios and drive strategic decisions.

  10. Invest in Continuous Training: Educate operational teams on how accurate data entry at the source (like logging energy bills in S/4HANA) impacts global compliance.

Why S/4HANA API Automation Matters

The Architecture Reality: Transitioning to automated S/4HANA APIs is non-negotiable for establishing a finance-grade "Green Ledger." It eliminates human errors inherent in manual spreadsheet updates, secures automated data lineage for third-party auditors, and enables continuous, real-time performance steering rather than lagging retrospective reporting.

entry.title: Re: Custom “Final Salary” field – how to derive cumulativ...
entry.content:
This is expected behavior.  For example, if you have 5 employees and each receive a 3% increase, the calculated total function would in fact produce 15%, not the 3% you would expect.  the total is a summation of the column not a calculation within the total row.

My thought for getting a percentage would be to use a budget.  You could either have the proposed salary compared to the current salary or use the % of a custom field.   That way you could see the percentage increase in total.   I think you may need to configure and then tweak to get exactly what you want.

entry.title: What is the best SAP ECC object to separately report a small business within an existing CoCode?
entry.content:
We are running SAP ECC with a Company Code based reporting model, where one Company Code represents one Reporting Unit.

Within one of our existing Company Codes, we have two very small business activities that are legally required to be reported separately (not separate legal entity but the business must be reported separately to the regional tax office where it is located). The volume is very low (approximately 20 FI documents per month for each business), so creating separate Company Codes is difficult to justify.

We are looking for a simple and pragmatic way to identify and report Balance Sheet and P&L items belonging to these businesses.

We would prefer to avoid implementing larger solutions such as:

  • Profit Center Accounting
  • Segment Reporting with document splitting and zero-balancing
  • Any other complex reporting architecture

Instead, we are looking for a standard SAP ECC field or organizational object that could be maintained manually on selected postings and later used for reporting purposes.

Has anyone faced a similar requirement?

Which standard SAP object would you recommend in this scenario?

entry.title: AI Integration Query for Crystal Reports Templates

We now want to customize these forms, lists, and templates specifically for our company and manage them directly within SAP. Controlling:

My current understanding:

  • G/L Accounts and Chart of Accounts cannot be directly replicated to S/4 Public Cloud via DRFOUT
  • SAP recommends using Master Data Integration (MDI) for Finance objects

entry.title: Re: What is the best practice for creating Communication...
entry.content:
Dear @Moon3,

For SAP_COM_0466, we should not create a new communication arrangement only because this is a rollout branch/company. SAP_COM_0466 is the technical communication scenario for Printing Pull Integration, used for the connection between SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP Cloud Print Manager / print queues so the decision should be based on the printing infrastructure and security ownership, not on the company code

More details below:

  • SAP Learning – Preparing Printing Scenario Pull Integration in the Cloud Tenant
  • SAP Help – Output Correspondence

If the branch uses the same SAP Cloud Print Manager / same central printing infrastructure as headquarters, the existing setup can be reused. Branch-specific printing should then be controlled through separate print queues and output/printer determination. SAP confirms that several queues can be connected to the same SAP Cloud Print Manager, provided the queues use the same communication user.

More details below:

  • SAP Help – Creating Print Queues

A separate communication arrangement or communication user should be created only if the branch requires technical separation, for example a separate Cloud Print Manager instance, separate credentials/certificate governance, separate local print server, separate support ownership or the ability to deactivate branch printing independently from headquarters

Note that a separate SAP Cloud Print Manager instance technically requires a separate communication user, as it is not supported to connect to the same runtime system with the same communication user multiple times

More details below:

  • SAP Help – Setting Up the SAP Cloud Print Manager for Pull Integration

Summary:

Reuse the existing SAP_COM_0466 setup for a shared central printing infrastructure. Create a separate setup only when there is a real technical or governance separation

Best Regards,

Dawid

  • The recipient can see the instances in the “My Situations - Extended” app.
  • However, no notification is shown on the SAP Fiori launchpad / start page.

  • A situation instance definitely exists, so the issue is not the instance creation itself. According to the troubleshooting guidance, if an instance exists but no notification was sent, the next step is to check the notification / lifecycle settings.
  • In the trigger conditions, the first/opening condition uses the lifecycle option “Notify all recipients when a situation is created…”, which should be the correct setting for initial notification.
  • Recipient determination seems to work at least for situation visibility, because the user can see the situation instance in “My Situations - Extended”. SAP also recommends first checking whether the instance exists there.
  • The global notification infrastructure seems to be active, because the same user receives launchpad notifications for another situation type (MRP). Also, the notification service and push channel are active in the system (/IWNGW/NOTIFICATION and NOTIFICATIONPUSHAPC).

Thanks in advance!
