entry.title: Talk to SAP System in Plain English: Building an S/4HANA Analyst with Pi SDK and SAP AI Core
entry.content:
Pi SDK in Action: Build an SAP S/4HANA AI Analyst

What Are We Building?

A CLI tool that answers natural-language questions about SAP ERP data. Ask a question, and Pi figures out which APIs to call, chains the lookups, and returns the answer.

 

$ pnpm start

SAP S/4HANA Analyst ready. Ask anything about your ERP data.

You: Show me 3 purchase orders with their supplier names

  [reading] erp-analyst/SKILL.md
  [querying] PurchaseOrder top 3
  [querying] A_Supplier (filter: Supplier eq '17300001')
Here are 3 purchase orders with their supplier names:

- 4500000001 - Domestic US Supplier 1 (17300001)
- 4500000002 - Domestic US Supplier 1 (17300001)
- 4500000003 - Domestic US Supplier 1 (17300001)


Three steps, chained automatically. You did not write the chain.

In Blog 2, you built a similar agent with LangChain - ReAct loops, state graphs, message history, all wired by hand. This blog gets the same outcome with a fundamentally different developer experience: hand Pi an LLM, a tool, and a Markdown file. Pi hands you back the running agent. One function call: session.prompt("your question"). No state machines. No graph nodes. No orchestration code.

If you have not read the Pi intro blog yet, start there.

We will build this incrementally. Each section teaches a Pi SDK concept (theory), then you apply it (lab).

Prerequisites

  • Read the Pi intro blog - you understand what Pi is and have it set up
  • Node.js 18+ - required for the Pi SDK
  • pnpm - package manager (npm install -g pnpm if you don't have it)
  • API access to SAP AI Core - same credentials from Blog 1 (AICORE_CLIENT_ID, AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET, etc.)

Step 1: Create the Project

Lab

mkdir pi-s4hana-analyst
cd pi-s4hana-analyst
pnpm init

Install dependencies:

pnpm add @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent @earendil-works/pi-ai typebox
pnpm add -D typescript tsx @types/node

What each dependency does:

  • @earendil-works/pi-coding-agent - Pi SDK: the agent runtime, session management, tool registration
  • @earendil-works/pi-ai - Pi's AI primitives: streaming protocol, message types, cost calculation
  • typebox - schema definitions for tool parameters (Pi uses TypeBox instead of Zod)
  • typescript - type checking (dev only)
  • tsx - runs TypeScript directly without a build step (dev only)
  • @types/node - Node.js type definitions (dev only)

Create tsconfig.json in your project root (next to package.json) :

{
  "compilerOptions": {
    "target": "ES2022",
    "module": "ESNext",
    "moduleResolution": "bundler",
    "strict": true,
    "esModuleInterop": true,
    "outDir": "dist",
    "rootDir": "src"
  },
  "include": ["src"]
}


Your package.json in the project root should now look like this (dependency versions may differ in the future). You need to make two changes: add "type": "module" and replace the default scripts section with a start script:

{
  "name": "pi-s4hana-analyst",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "module",
  "description": "",
  "main": "index.js",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "tsx src/main.ts"
  },
  "keywords": [],
  "author": "",
  "license": "ISC",
  "dependencies": {
    "@earendil-works/pi-ai": "^0.78.1",
    "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent": "^0.78.1",
    "typebox": "^1.2.2"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/node": "^25.9.2",
    "tsx": "^4.22.4",
    "typescript": "^6.0.3"
  }
}

The two changes from the default pnpm init output:

  1. Added "type": "module" - required for ESM imports (import syntax)
  2. Changed scripts to "start": "tsx src/main.ts" - runs our app with pnpm start

Create the folder structure:

mkdir src
mkdir .pi
mkdir .pi/skills
mkdir .pi/skills/erp-analyst

On macOS/Linux you can use mkdir -p src .pi/skills/erp-analyst as a shorthand.

Step 2: Get Your SAP API Business Hub Sandbox Key

Theory

For this blog, we query the SAP S/4HANA sandbox system hosted on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. This is a free, read-only system with pre-loaded demo data (purchase orders, business partners, sales orders, materials) that responds to real OData API requests. No SAP license required.

But this is just one example. The same pattern works for any data source: a live S/4HANA system, a SuccessFactors tenant, any REST API, a database, or local files. The tool is the only piece that changes.

Lab

Follow these steps to get your sandbox API key:

  1. Open the Purchase Order API page on SAP Business Accelerator Hub
  2. Log in with your SAP account (create a free one if you don't have one)
  3. Click Show API Key in the top right corner of the page
  4. Copy the key

This key works for all S/4HANA Cloud sandbox APIs on api.sap.com, not just the Purchase Order API. You only need one key.

Step 3: Configure Environment Variables

Theory

In Blog 1, you used the Python SDK (sap-ai-sdk-gen) to connect to SAP AI Core. Here we connect using standard OAuth 2.0 HTTP calls - the same mechanism the Python SDK uses internally.

The credentials are identical. The model is configurable via AICORE_MODEL.

Lab

Create .env in your project root with your SAP AI Core credentials from Blog 1 and the sandbox API key from the previous step:

AICORE_CLIENT_ID=sb-abc123-def456...
AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret...
AICORE_AUTH_URL=https://your-tenant.authentication.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
AICORE_BASE_URL=https://api.ai.prod.eu-central-1.aws.ml.hana.ondemand.com/v2
AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP=default
AICORE_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini
SAP_API_KEY=your-sandbox-api-key-from-step-2

Note: Set AICORE_MODEL to any OpenAI-compatible model deployed in your tenant (e.g. gpt-5, gpt-5-mini, gpt-4o-mini). Check your SAP AI Launchpad to see what is available. Anthropic and Gemini models require additional provider configuration not covered in this blog.

Create a .gitignore file in your project root with the following content:

.env
node_modules

Step 4: Define the Custom Tool

Theory

A tool is a function Pi's LLM can call. You define what arguments it accepts and what it returns. Pi decides when to call it based on the user's question.

You define tools with defineTool() and pass them to createAgentSession(). The LLM sees the tool's name, description, and parameter schema, and decides at runtime whether to invoke it.

Lab

Create the file src/tools.ts:

/**
 * SAP S/4HANA OData Query Tool
 *
 * Provides Pi's LLM with the ability to query SAP S/4HANA OData APIs.
 * The LLM controls which service, entity, filter, and fields to query.
 * This tool handles HTTP execution and response parsing.
 *
 * We use `servicePath` instead of just a service name because SAP
 * S/4HANA Cloud APIs use different URL structures depending on the
 * OData version: V2 APIs use /sap/opu/odata/sap/{service} while V4
 * APIs use longer paths like /sap/opu/odata4/sap/{id}/srvd_a2x/...
 * The skill routing table provides the correct path for each API.
 */

import { defineTool } from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";
import { Type } from "typebox";

const SAP_SANDBOX_HOST = "https://sandbox.api.sap.com/s4hanacloud";

export const s4hanaQueryTool = defineTool({
  name: "s4hana_query",
  label: "SAP S/4HANA Query",
  description:
    "Query SAP S/4HANA OData APIs. Use this to fetch purchase orders, " +
    "sales orders, business partners, materials, and other ERP data. " +
    "Returns JSON results from the SAP system.",
  parameters: Type.Object({
    servicePath: Type.String({
      description:
        "Full OData service path as provided in the skill routing table, " +
        "e.g. '/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER' (V2) or " +
        "'/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_purchaseorder_2/srvd_a2x/sap/purchaseorder/0001' (V4)",
    }),
    entity: Type.String({
      description:
        "Entity set name, e.g. 'PurchaseOrder' or 'A_BusinessPartner'",
    }),
    filter: Type.Optional(
      Type.String({
        description: "OData $filter expression, e.g. \"Supplier eq '17300001'\"",
      })
    ),
    select: Type.Optional(
      Type.String({
        description:
          "Comma-separated fields to return, e.g. 'PurchaseOrder,Supplier,CreationDate'",
      })
    ),
    top: Type.Optional(
      Type.Number({ description: "Max results to return (default: 10)" })
    ),
    expand: Type.Optional(
      Type.String({
        description: "Navigation properties to expand, e.g. '_PurchaseOrderItem'",
      })
    ),
  }),

  execute: async (_toolCallId, params, signal) => {
    const apiKey = process.env.SAP_API_KEY;
    if (!apiKey) {
      return {
        content: [{ type: "text" as const, text: "Error: SAP_API_KEY environment variable is not set." }],
        details: {},
        isError: true,
      };
    }

    const url = new URL(`${SAP_SANDBOX_HOST}${params.servicePath}/${params.entity}`);
    url.searchParams.set("$format", "json");
    url.searchParams.set("$top", String(params.top ?? 10));
    if (params.filter) url.searchParams.set("$filter", params.filter);
    if (params.select) url.searchParams.set("$select", params.select);
    if (params.expand) url.searchParams.set("$expand", params.expand);

    try {
      const response = await fetch(url.toString(), {
        headers: { APIKey: apiKey, Accept: "application/json" },
        signal,
      });

      if (!response.ok) {
        const errorText = await response.text();
        return {
          content: [{ type: "text" as const, text: `SAP API error (${response.status}): ${errorText.slice(0, 500)}` }],
          details: { status: response.status },
          isError: true,
        };
      }

      const data = await response.json();
      const results = data.d?.results ?? data.d ?? data.value ?? data;
      const count = Array.isArray(results) ? results.length : 1;

      return {
        content: [{ type: "text" as const, text: JSON.stringify(results, null, 2).slice(0, 15000) }],
        details: { count, servicePath: params.servicePath, entity: params.entity },
      };
    } catch (error) {
      const message = error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error);
      return {
        content: [{ type: "text" as const, text: `Request failed: ${message}` }],
        details: {},
        isError: true,
      };
    }
  },
});

What this file does:

  • Defines one tool called s4hana_query that the LLM can call whenever it needs SAP data
  • Accepts parameters that the LLM fills in: which API path, which entity, what filters, which fields
  • Builds an OData URL from those parameters and makes the HTTP request
  • Handles both V2 and V4 responses - V2 returns data.d.results, V4 returns data.value
  • Returns errors gracefully with isError: true so the LLM knows something went wrong and can adjust
  • Passes the abort signal to fetch() so Ctrl+C propagates correctly

The tool knows nothing about when to query which API. That is the skill's job.

Step 5: The Skill

Theory

The tool gives Pi the ability to call SAP APIs. But SAP S/4HANA has hundreds of OData services, each with dozens of entities. Without guidance, the LLM would have to guess which service and entity to query for "show me purchase orders."

A skill provides procedural knowledge: step-by-step instructions for how to solve specific categories of problems. Pi loads skills on-demand - only the name and description sit in the system prompt. The full content loads when the LLM decides the skill is relevant.

Lab

Create the file .pi/skills/erp-analyst/SKILL.md:

---
name: erp-analyst
description: "Analyze SAP S/4HANA ERP data including purchase orders, sales orders, business partners, materials, and accounting documents. Use this skill when the user asks about procurement, sales, suppliers, customers, inventory, or any ERP transactional data."
---

# SAP S/4HANA ERP Analysis

You have access to a live SAP S/4HANA system via the `s4hana_query` tool.

## Query Routing Table

| Business Question | Service Path | Entity | Useful Fields |
|-------------------|-------------|--------|---------------|
| Purchase orders | `/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_purchaseorder_2/srvd_a2x/sap/purchaseorder/0001` | `PurchaseOrder` | PurchaseOrder, Supplier, PurchaseOrderDate, CompanyCode, PurchasingOrganization |
| PO line items | `/sap/opu/odata4/sap/api_purchaseorder_2/srvd_a2x/sap/purchaseorder/0001` | `PurchaseOrderItem` | PurchaseOrder, PurchaseOrderItem, Material, OrderQuantity, NetPriceAmount |
| Sales orders | `/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SALES_ORDER_SRV` | `A_SalesOrder` | SalesOrder, SoldToParty, CreationDate, TotalNetAmount |
| SO line items | `/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_SALES_ORDER_SRV` | `A_SalesOrderItem` | SalesOrder, SalesOrderItem, Material, RequestedQuantity, NetAmount |
| Business partners | `/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER` | `A_BusinessPartner` | BusinessPartner, BusinessPartnerName, BusinessPartnerCategory |
| Suppliers | `/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER` | `A_Supplier` | Supplier, SupplierName |
| Products/materials | `/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_PRODUCT_SRV` | `A_Product` | Product, ProductType, BaseUnit, ProductGroup |

> **Note:** Purchase Order uses OData V4 (newer API). The others use OData V2. The tool handles both formats automatically.

## OData Filter Syntax

- String equality: `Supplier eq '17300001'`
- Date comparison: `CreationDate ge datetime'2025-01-01T00:00:00'`
- Numeric: `NetPriceAmount gt 1000`
- Logical: `Supplier eq '17300001' and PurchasingOrganization eq '17'`

## Analysis Rules

1. Always use `$select` - request only the fields you need.
2. Set a reasonable `$top` - default to 10, increase only if asked.
3. Chain queries when needed - for "top suppliers by PO count", first fetch POs, then resolve names.
4. Summarize, don't dump - present totals, top-N, trends.
5. If no results, tell the user clearly. Don't retry with speculative filters.

What this file does:

  • The frontmatter (name and description) is all Pi loads at startup. It goes into the system prompt so the LLM knows this skill exists.
  • The routing table tells the LLM exactly which API path and entity to use for each business question. Without this, the LLM would guess.
  • The analysis rules define behavioral constraints so the LLM queries efficiently and does not loop endlessly.

Pi loads the full content of this file only when the LLM decides it is relevant to the user's question. This is progressive disclosure - zero context wasted until needed.

Step 6: Project Context with AGENTS.md

Theory

AGENTS.md provides standing instructions that are always in Pi's context. Unlike skills (loaded on-demand), this is always present. Keep it short and focused on overall behavior.

Lab

Create the file AGENTS.md in the project root:

# SAP S/4HANA Analyst

You are an SAP ERP data analyst. Your job is to answer business questions about procurement, sales, finance, and master data by querying the SAP S/4HANA system.

## Behavior

- Always use the `erp-analyst` skill to determine which API to call.
- Use the `s4hana_query` tool for all SAP data access.
- Present results in a clear, structured format (tables, bullet points, summaries).
- When the user asks a follow-up, use context from the conversation.
- If you are unsure which API to use, explain what you would need to know.
- Never make more than 3 tool calls for a single question. If no results after 3 attempts, report what you found (or that no data was found) and stop.
- Do not ask the user clarifying questions. Use sensible defaults and proceed.

## Environment

- The SAP_API_KEY environment variable provides access to the S/4HANA sandbox.
- The system has demo data for company codes 1010 and 1710.
- The sandbox contains demo data from 2016. When the user asks for "recent" or "last N months", query without date filters or use dates from 2016.
- All data is read-only.

What this file does:

  • Tells Pi its role (ERP analyst)
  • Sets behavioral rules (use the skill, use the tool, don't ask questions, limit retries)
  • Documents the environment (sandbox data is from 2016, read-only, available company codes)

Step 7: The SAP AI Core Provider

Theory

Pi supports many LLM providers out of the box (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google, etc.). But in the SAP ecosystem, your models run on SAP AI Core. Pi does not know how to talk to SAP AI Core natively - you teach it by registering a custom provider.

The good news: SAP AI Core's Azure-backed models (GPT-4o, GPT-5) expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Pi has a built-in openai-completions streaming implementation that handles all the hard parts (SSE parsing, tool calls, retries, edge cases). We just need to point it at the right URL with the right token.

Our provider does three things:

  1. Gets an OAuth token from SAP AI Core (with caching so we don't re-authenticate every call)
  2. Discovers deployments - finds which deployment ID corresponds to the model name you configured in AICORE_MODEL
  3. Registers the models with Pi using api: "openai-completions" so Pi's built-in streaming handles everything

No custom SSE parsing. No streaming code. Pi does it all.

Lab

Create the file src/sap-ai-core-provider.ts:

/**
 * SAP AI Core Provider for Pi SDK
 *
 * Registers SAP AI Core as a custom LLM provider using Pi's built-in
 * OpenAI-compatible streaming. No custom streaming code needed.
 *
 * This provider:
 * - Authenticates via OAuth 2.0 client credentials
 * - Discovers the deployment for the configured model
 * - Registers it with Pi's built-in openai-completions API
 *
 * Supports OpenAI-compatible models (GPT family) deployed on SAP AI Core.
 * For Anthropic (Claude) or Gemini models, a different api type and
 * endpoint format would be needed.
 */

import type { Api } from "@earendil-works/pi-ai";
import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";

const PROVIDER_ID = "sap-ai-core";

export async function createSapAiCoreProvider(): Promise<{ factory: (pi: ExtensionAPI) => void; deploymentBaseUrl: string }> {
  const required = ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID", "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET", "AICORE_AUTH_URL", "AICORE_BASE_URL", "AICORE_MODEL"];
  const missing = required.filter((k) => !process.env[k]);
  if (missing.length > 0) {
    throw new Error(`SAP AI Core configuration missing: ${missing.join(", ")}. Check your .env file.`);
  }

  const modelId = process.env.AICORE_MODEL!;
  const baseUrl = process.env.AICORE_BASE_URL!;
  const resourceGroup = process.env.AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP || "default";

  const token = await fetchAccessToken();
  const deploymentId = await resolveDeploymentId(token, modelId, baseUrl, resourceGroup);
  const deploymentBaseUrl = `${baseUrl}/inference/deployments/${deploymentId}/v1`;

  const factory = (pi: ExtensionAPI) => {
    pi.registerProvider(PROVIDER_ID, {
      baseUrl: deploymentBaseUrl,
      apiKey: token,
      api: "openai-completions" as Api,
      headers: { "AI-Resource-Group": resourceGroup },
      models: [{
        id: modelId,
        name: `${modelId} (SAP AI Core)`,
        reasoning: true,
        input: ["text", "image"] as ("text" | "image")[],
        cost: { input: 0, output: 0, cacheRead: 0, cacheWrite: 0 },
        contextWindow: 128000,
        maxTokens: 16384,
        compat: { maxTokensField: "max_completion_tokens" as any },
      }],
    });
  };

  return { factory, deploymentBaseUrl };
}

async function fetchAccessToken(): Promise<string> {
  const clientId = process.env.AICORE_CLIENT_ID!;
  const clientSecret = process.env.AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET!;
  const authUrl = process.env.AICORE_AUTH_URL!;
  const tokenUrl = authUrl.includes("/oauth/token") ? authUrl : `${authUrl}/oauth/token`;

  const credentials = Buffer.from(`${clientId}:${clientSecret}`).toString("base64");
  const response = await fetch(`${tokenUrl}?grant_type=client_credentials`, {
    method: "POST",
    headers: {
      Authorization: `Basic ${credentials}`,
      "Content-Type": "application/x-www-form-urlencoded",
    },
  });

  if (!response.ok) {
    const error = await response.text();
    throw new Error(`OAuth token request failed (${response.status}): ${error}`);
  }

  const data = (await response.json()) as { access_token: string; expires_in: number };
  return data.access_token;
}

async function resolveDeploymentId(
  token: string,
  modelName: string,
  baseUrl: string,
  resourceGroup: string,
): Promise<string> {
  const response = await fetch(`${baseUrl}/lm/deployments?$top=100&status=RUNNING`, {
    headers: {
      Authorization: `Bearer ${token}`,
      "AI-Resource-Group": resourceGroup,
    },
  });

  if (!response.ok) {
    const error = await response.text();
    throw new Error(`Failed to list deployments (${response.status}): ${error}`);
  }

  const data = (await response.json()) as {
    resources: Array<{
      id: string;
      status: string;
      details?: { resources?: { backendDetails?: { model?: { name?: string } } } };
    }>;
  };

  const deployments = data.resources
    .filter((r) => r.status === "RUNNING")
    .map((r) => ({ id: r.id, modelName: r.details?.resources?.backendDetails?.model?.name ?? "" }))
    .filter((d) => d.modelName);

  const found = deployments.find((d) => d.modelName === modelName);
  if (!found) {
    const available = deployments.map((d) => d.modelName).join(", ");
    throw new Error(
      `Model '${modelName}' has no running deployment in your SAP AI Core tenant.\n` +
      `Available models: ${available}\n` +
      `Update AICORE_MODEL in your .env to one of the available models.`
    );
  }

  return found.id;
}

What this file does:

  • createSapAiCoreProvider() - validates credentials, fetches an OAuth token, discovers the deployment for your configured model, and returns a factory that registers it with Pi. If the model has no deployment, throws a clear error listing what IS available.
  • fetchAccessToken() - gets an OAuth token using client credentials (same auth the Python SDK does internally)
  • resolveDeploymentId() - lists running deployments and finds the one matching your model name. Deployment IDs are dynamic (they change on re-deploy), so we resolve them at startup rather than hardcoding.

The key insight: SAP AI Core's Azure-backed models (GPT-4o, GPT-5, GPT-5-mini) expose an OpenAI-compatible endpoint at /inference/deployments/{id}/v1/chat/completions. By setting api: "openai-completions", Pi handles all streaming, tool calling, and edge cases using its own battle-tested implementation.

Important limitation: This provider works ONLY for OpenAI-compatible models deployed on SAP AI Core (GPT family). If you need to use Anthropic Claude models (deployed via AWS Bedrock) or Google Gemini models (deployed via Vertex AI), they use completely different endpoint formats and streaming protocols. You would need a separate provider registration with api: "anthropic-messages" for Claude or a custom streamSimple for Gemini. The architecture pattern stays the same - you just register additional providers for each backend type.

Step 8: Wire It All Together

Theory

createAgentSessionServices() + createAgentSessionFromServices() is the recommended pattern for programmatic Pi SDK usage with custom providers. It ensures extensions are registered at the right point in the initialization lifecycle.

Lab

Create the file src/session.ts:

/**
 * Agent Session Configuration
 *
 * Brings together the SAP AI Core provider, the S/4HANA query tool,
 * and Pi's automatic skill/context discovery into one session.
 */

import type { Api } from "@earendil-works/pi-ai";
import {
  createAgentSessionFromServices,
  createAgentSessionServices,
  SessionManager,
  SettingsManager,
  type AgentSession,
} from "@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent";
import { createSapAiCoreProvider } from "./sap-ai-core-provider.js";
import { s4hanaQueryTool } from "./tools.js";

const PROVIDER_ID = "sap-ai-core";

export async function createAnalystSession(): Promise<AgentSession> {
  const modelId = process.env.AICORE_MODEL!;

  const settingsManager = SettingsManager.inMemory({
    compaction: { enabled: false },
    retry: { enabled: true, maxRetries: 3 },
  });

  const { factory, deploymentBaseUrl } = await createSapAiCoreProvider();

  const services = await createAgentSessionServices({
    cwd: process.cwd(),
    settingsManager,
    resourceLoaderOptions: {
      extensionFactories: [factory],
    },
  });

  const { session } = await createAgentSessionFromServices({
    services,
    sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),
    customTools: [s4hanaQueryTool],
    tools: ["read", "s4hana_query"],
  });

  await session.setModel({
    id: modelId,
    name: `${modelId} (SAP AI Core)`,
    provider: PROVIDER_ID,
    api: "openai-completions" as Api,
    baseUrl: deploymentBaseUrl,
    reasoning: true,
    input: ["text", "image"] as ("text" | "image")[],
    cost: { input: 0, output: 0, cacheRead: 0, cacheWrite: 0 },
    contextWindow: 128000,
    maxTokens: 16384,
  });

  return session;
}

What this file does:

  • createSapAiCoreProvider() - returns the extension factory and the deployment URL. The factory registers our model with Pi. The URL is needed for setModel().
  • createAgentSessionServices() - creates Pi's internal services with our extension factory. This is where skills and AGENTS.md are auto-discovered from the working directory.
  • createAgentSessionFromServices() - creates the actual session with our custom tool and built-in read tool (needed for skill loading)
  • tools: ["read", "s4hana_query"] - explicitly lists which tools are active. If you omit your custom tool from this list, the LLM cannot call it.
  • session.setModel() - selects the model with the full object including api and baseUrl so Pi knows which streaming implementation to use

What to know about sessions

In this demo, we use a single in-memory session for simplicity. Here is what that means and what you would change for production:

  • Session lifetime. Demo: one session for the entire app runtime, all prompts share the same conversation history. Production: same, or create new sessions per user/request.
  • Memory. Demo: SessionManager.inMemory() - everything lost when app stops. Production: SessionManager.create(process.cwd()) - persists to disk, survives restarts.
  • Context window. Demo: grows with every turn until the model's limit (e.g. 128K tokens for GPT-5). Production: enable compaction with compaction: { enabled: true } - Pi auto-summarizes old messages when context reaches around 80%.
  • New session. Demo: only when you restart the app. Production: call runtime.newSession() programmatically, or use createAgentSessionRuntime for full session lifecycle management.

Conversation memory works automatically within a session. If you ask "List 3 suppliers" and then ask "Tell me more about the first one", the LLM sees the full conversation history and understands what "the first one" refers to.

Step 9: The Chat Loop

Theory

With the session created, you interact with Pi through:

  • session.prompt(text) - send a message and wait for the full response
  • session.subscribe(listener) - receive streaming events as they happen

The official Pi SDK event pattern:

session.subscribe((event) => {
  switch (event.type) {
    case "message_update":
      if (event.assistantMessageEvent.type === "text_delta") {
        process.stdout.write(event.assistantMessageEvent.delta);
      }
      break;
    case "tool_execution_start":
      console.log(`Tool: ${event.toolName}`);
      break;
    case "tool_execution_end":
      console.log(`Result: ${event.isError ? "error" : "done"}`);
      break;
  }
});

This gives the user real-time visibility into what Pi is doing: thinking, calling tools, streaming text.

Lab

Create the file src/main.ts:

/**
 * SAP S/4HANA AI Analyst - Entry Point
 *
 * Interactive chat loop that sends natural language questions
 * to Pi and streams the agent's responses in real time.
 */

import * as readline from "node:readline";
import { readFileSync } from "node:fs";
import { createAnalystSession } from "./session.js";

loadEnvFile();

async function main(): Promise<void> {
  console.log("\n  SAP S/4HANA Analyst");
  console.log("  Ask natural language questions about your ERP data.");
  console.log("  Type 'quit' to exit.\n");

  const session = await createAnalystSession();

  const rl = readline.createInterface({ input: process.stdin, output: process.stdout });
  const ask = (): Promise<string> => new Promise((resolve) => rl.question("You: ", resolve));

  try {
    while (true) {
      const input = (await ask()).trim();
      if (!input) continue;
      if (["quit", "exit", "q"].includes(input.toLowerCase())) {
        console.log("Goodbye!");
        break;
      }

      console.log();

      const unsub = session.subscribe((event) => {
        switch (event.type) {
          case "message_update":
            if (event.assistantMessageEvent.type === "text_delta") {
              process.stdout.write(event.assistantMessageEvent.delta);
            }
            if (event.assistantMessageEvent.type === "thinking_delta") {
              process.stdout.write(event.assistantMessageEvent.delta);
            }
            break;
          case "tool_execution_start": {
            const args = event.args as Record<string, unknown>;
            if (event.toolName === "read") {
              const path = String(args.path || "").split("/").slice(-2).join("/");
              console.log(`  [reading] ${path}`);
            } else if (event.toolName === "s4hana_query") {
              const entity = args.entity || "?";
              const filter = args.filter ? ` (filter: ${args.filter})` : "";
              const top = args.top ? ` top ${args.top}` : "";
              console.log(`  [querying] ${entity}${top}${filter}`);
            } else {
              console.log(`  [${event.toolName}]`);
            }
            break;
          }
          case "tool_execution_end":
            if (event.isError) {
              console.log(`  [error] ${event.toolName} failed`);
            }
            break;
        }
      });

      await session.prompt(input);
      unsub();
      console.log("\n");
    }
  } finally {
    rl.close();
    session.dispose();
  }
}

function loadEnvFile(): void {
  try {
    const content = readFileSync(".env", "utf-8");
    for (const line of content.split("\n")) {
      const trimmed = line.trim();
      if (!trimmed || trimmed.startsWith("#")) continue;
      const eqIndex = trimmed.indexOf("=");
      if (eqIndex === -1) continue;
      const key = trimmed.slice(0, eqIndex).trim();
      let value = trimmed.slice(eqIndex + 1).trim();
      if ((value.startsWith("'") && value.endsWith("'")) || (value.startsWith('"') && value.endsWith('"'))) {
        value = value.slice(1, -1);
      }
      if (!process.env[key]) process.env[key] = value;
    }
  } catch {
    /* .env is optional */
  }
}

main().catch(console.error);

What this file does:

  • loadEnvFile() - reads .env at startup, strips surrounding quotes from values (Python's pydantic does this automatically, we need to do it manually)
  • session.subscribe() - subscribes to streaming events for real-time output. The user sees tool calls as they happen and text as it streams token by token.
  • unsub() - unsubscribes after each prompt completes so events from the next prompt don't duplicate
  • session.dispose() - cleans up resources when the app exits

Step 10: Run It

At this point, your project should look like this:

pi-s4hana-analyst/
+-- package.json
+-- tsconfig.json
+-- .env
+-- .gitignore
+-- AGENTS.md
+-- .pi/
|   +-- skills/
|       +-- erp-analyst/
|           +-- SKILL.md
+-- src/
    +-- main.ts
    +-- session.ts
    +-- sap-ai-core-provider.ts
    +-- tools.ts

If any file is missing, go back to the step that creates it.

pnpm start

Try these prompts:

You: List 3 suppliers from SAP

You: Show me 3 purchase orders with their supplier names

You: What products are available?

The second prompt demonstrates multi-step reasoning. Watch the events:

  [reading] erp-analyst/SKILL.md
  [querying] PurchaseOrder top 3
  [querying] A_Supplier (filter: Supplier eq '17300001')
Here are 3 purchase orders with their supplier names:
- 4500000001 - Domestic US Supplier 1 (17300001)
- 4500000002 - Domestic US Supplier 1 (17300001)
- 4500000003 - Domestic US Supplier 1 (17300001)

Pi chained three steps automatically:

  1. Read the skill (learned the routing table)
  2. Queried Purchase Orders (got supplier IDs)
  3. Queried Business Partners (resolved supplier names)

You did not program this chain. The skill provided the routing knowledge, and Pi's agent loop handled the orchestration.

What Just Happened?

Let's compare what you wrote vs what Pi did for you:

What you wrote:

  • A tool (~100 lines) - what the agent can do
  • A skill (~40 lines of Markdown) - what the agent knows
  • A provider (~150 lines) - which LLM to use
  • A session setup (~60 lines) - wiring
  • A chat loop (~80 lines) - UX

What Pi handled automatically:

  • The reasoning loop (when to call tools, when to respond)
  • Multi-step tool chaining (calling multiple APIs to answer one question)
  • Context management (fitting conversation + tool results in the context window)
  • Streaming (token-by-token response delivery)
  • Error recovery (retry on transient failures)
  • Skill loading (on-demand, only when relevant)
  • Conversation memory (follow-up questions understand previous context)

Adapt This Pattern

The same architecture works for any use case. You only change three things:

  • The tool (what to call). Swap the SAP OData tool for a SuccessFactors client, a Concur expense reader, an Ariba spend connector, or any other API.
  • The skill (what to know). Replace the ERP routing table with finance reporting rules, HR policy lookups, procurement workflows, or whatever domain knowledge your agent needs.
  • The provider (which LLM). Point at a different SAP AI Core deployment, a different model, or a different tenant.

The session setup, the chat loop, and Pi's orchestration stay identical. That is the value of Pi SDK: you define capabilities and knowledge, Pi provides the runtime.

What's Next?

You wrote a tool, a skill, a provider, and a thin chat loop. Pi did the orchestration. Swap the tool and the skill, and you have a SuccessFactors agent, a Concur auditor, an Ariba analyzer. Same harness, different domain.

A few directions to take it:

  • Persist conversations - swap SessionManager.inMemory() for SessionManager.create(process.cwd())
  • Enable compaction - set compaction: { enabled: true } so Pi auto-summarizes when context fills
  • Add Claude or Gemini - register a second provider with api: "anthropic-messages" (Bedrock) or a custom streamSimple (Vertex AI)
  • Add a web UI - wrap the session in FastAPI and put a SAPUI5 frontend on top, the same way Blog 3 did for the LangGraph agent

The architecture stays the same. You give Pi an LLM, tools, and skills. Pi gives you back an agent.

Resources

  • Pi documentation
  • Mario Zechner on Pi's design philosophy
  • Source code for this blog

entry.title: Re: Rate Limiting on SAP Sales & Service Cloud V2 APIs - Must to know
entry.content:
What happens when the API usage limit of 150,000 requests per day, along with the per-user limit of 1,500 requests per day, is reached?

Will administrators receive a notification when the limit is exceeded or about to be reached? Additionally, is there an option or roadmap to increase these limit if higher usage is required?

entry.title: Duplicate Official Document Number (FT) in FI-CA Invoicing
entry.content:
Hello,

We are facing an issue in FI-CA / Convergent Invoicing where the same Official Document Number (FT…) is being assigned to different Invoice Documents, and proforma numbers also appear to be duplicated or incorrectly reused.

Example:

  • Invoice Document: 420000008914
  • Official Document Number: FT092300145672
  • Related proforma / invoicing orders: SGCE310500882134 / SGCN310500774219

It looks like both the FT number and the proforma number assignment are not behaving correctly during the invoicing process.

Has anyone seen this before and knows the root cause?

Is it typically related to:

  • number range configuration?
  • parallel invoicing jobs?
  • periodic invoicing setup?

And what is the standard way to correct and prevent this behavior?

Thanks in advance.

entry.title: Claude Generates So Much — Here's How I Actually Keep Up
entry.content:
Claude Generates So Much — Here's How I Actually Keep Up

AI is producing an almost comical amount of output these days. In a single session, Claude can scaffold a feature, write the tests, refactor two files, explain the architecture, and suggest three follow-up improvements — all before you've finished your coffee. It's genuinely impressive. It's also genuinely hard to follow.

I kept running into the same pattern: a long session ends, something real was built, and I broadly understood what it does — but the why behind the design decisions, the trade-offs that were made, the subtle things that matter when the code eventually breaks? Hazy at best. I'd scroll back through the chat, skim a few explanations, tell myself I'd review it properly later. I never reviewed it properly later.

So I built a Claude Code skill that turns any session — or any topic — into a short, focused, interactive learning session. The kind where you actually have to demonstrate understanding before moving on. It's called teach-me.

You run it right after a session while the context is still fresh: /teach-me this PR, /teach-me what we just built, /teach-me the migration pipeline. It works just as well for any concept you want to properly understand — not just things Claude built for you.

The problem with asking AI to teach you things

Here's the thing about reading a great explanation: it feels like understanding. The words make sense. The logic tracks. You nod. You say "ah yes, exactly." You close the tab. Two hours later a colleague asks you about it and you stare at them like they just asked you to explain quantum physics in Klingon.

This is not a Claude problem. It's a cognitive load problem. It's the same reason you can read a textbook chapter and feel smart, then fail the exam. Passive reception isn't learning. Your brain files "I saw this" next to "I understand this" — and those are two completely different drawers.

The standard AI tutoring loop goes like this:

  1. You ask Claude to explain something
  2. Claude writes a wall of text in the chat
  3. You scroll through it
  4. You say "thanks"
  5. You forget 90% of it within the hour

I'm being uncharitable. But only a little. The underlying issue is that nothing in this loop requires you to do anything with the information. And things you don't do anything with don't stick.

The idea: an AI that refuses to move on

I wanted Claude to teach me the way a good engineer onboards a junior: not just explaining, but checking. "Okay, in your own words — what does this component actually do?" And then waiting for the answer. And then filling in exactly the gaps that are missing, not re-explaining everything from scratch.

The teach-me skill turns any topic into a structured three-phase learning session with actual gates you have to pass before moving on.

The three phases are always:

  1. The Problem — why does this thing exist? what was hard before it?
  2. The Solution — why this specific approach? what were the design decisions?
  3. Broader Context — why does it matter? what else does it touch?

Before each phase, Claude asks you to explain your current understanding. It takes what's right and fills in exactly what's missing. Then it generates a styled HTML lesson file that opens in your browser. You read it, take a quiz, and report your score back in chat. Score under 80%? Claude reteaches the specific concepts you missed — and a "Retry quiz" button in the browser resets the quiz without regenerating the whole file. Score over 80%? You advance. No skipping. Not even if you say "I get it, trust me."

"Never advance a phase without passing the gate — not even if the user says 'I get it, move on.'"
— literally a rule in the skill's source code

I built that rule in specifically because I knew I would try to talk my way past it.

What an actual session looks like

I ran a session on the skill itself — very meta, I know — to test the Full mode. I typed:

/teach-me this session

Claude asked which mode: Full mode (HTML lesson in browser) or Simple mode (chat only). I picked Full.

Then came the restate gate. Before generating anything, Claude asked me to explain in my own words why the original chat-only skill fell short. My answer was roughly: "it was not fancy enough, humans learn better with visually appealing materials."

Claude took what was right (visual structure helps) and filled in what I missed: the cognitive load framing (reading prose in chat requires your brain to parse layout and content simultaneously), the AskUserQuestion limitations (4-option cap, modal blocking, no persistence), and the lack of session persistence. Two sentences of targeted gap-fill instead of a five-paragraph re-explanation.

Then it generated phase-1.html and opened it in my browser. The lesson had structured sections, an "Explain it simpler" toggle (ELI5 / ELI14 / ELI Intern), a sidebar tracking which concepts I was covering, and a 4-question quiz at the bottom.

I scored 2/4. Missed Q2 and Q4.

I reported back in chat: "Score: 2/4. Missed: Q2, Q4"

Claude reteached those two concepts in a short paragraph. No HTML regeneration. No waiting for a new file to open. I clicked "Retry quiz" in the already-open browser tab, got fresh questions on the same page, scored 3/4. Passed.

The HTML lesson format

The lesson pages are self-contained HTML files — no server, no build step, no npm. Just a file you can open today and revisit six months from now.

Each page has:

  • A header with the topic name, phase label, and a three-dot progress indicator
  • A two-column layout: lesson content on the left, a sticky "Concepts this phase" sidebar on the right
  • Support for code blocks and inline diagrams
  • An "Explain it simpler" accordion with three tabs: ELI5, ELI14, ELI Intern
  • A quiz section at the bottom — same column width as the lesson
  • A "Retry quiz" button that resets JS quiz state in-browser without reloading the file
  • A "Copy score to clipboard" button so you can paste your result straight into chat

Sessions are saved in teach-me-sessions/{slug}-{YYYYMMDD}/ alongside a session.md checklist. If you close Claude Code and come back tomorrow, the skill detects the existing session and asks if you want to resume or start fresh.

How it works under the hood

Claude Code skills are Markdown files with YAML frontmatter. That's it. No SDK, no framework, no deployment. You put a SKILL.md in ~/.claude/skills/teach-me/, and the skill becomes available as /teach-me in any Claude Code session.

The skill file contains:

  • A description of the full flow as a Graphviz dot diagram (Claude can read these)
  • Explicit rules for quiz generation — never put the correct answer at index 0, vary positions, explain why wrong answers are wrong
  • Step-by-step instructions for each phase with exact bash commands to open the HTML
  • A "Common Mistakes" table listing things Claude would naturally do wrong — like skipping mode selection or advancing without a passing score

The HTML template lives alongside the skill. Claude reads it as reference and generates the actual lesson file from scratch, filling in real lesson content and quiz data per topic. The quiz engine is about 80 lines of vanilla JS.

The whole thing — skill file plus template — is around 400 lines total. No dependencies.

The reteach loop

When you fail a quiz:

  1. Report score and missed questions in chat
  2. Claude reteaches those specific concepts — in chat, concisely
  3. Click "Retry quiz" in the open browser tab (JS resets state, no file reload)
  4. Repeat up to 3 total attempts
  5. After 3 attempts without passing: Claude offers to re-explain from scratch, or you move on voluntarily

The 3-attempt cap exists because indefinite retry loops are demoralizing and usually mean the lesson needs restructuring, not more retaking. During my session, Phase 3 hit the cap — I kept missing a question about the "static HTML output pattern." The skill invoked the cap, offered me the choice, I said "move on," and we finished the session. No judgment. The checklist marked it done anyway.

The restate gate

The gate before each phase is the part that feels most like actual learning. You have to say something first. Even if it's wrong. Even if it's "I have no idea." Claude then calibrates the explanation to exactly what you're missing — instead of re-explaining things you already know.

Try it yourself

The skill is open source and available at: github.com/Dimi82/claude-playground-explorer

git clone https://github.com/Dimi82/claude-playground-explorer.git
cd claude-playground-explorer

mkdir -p ~/.claude/skills
ln -sf "$(pwd)/teach-me" ~/.claude/skills/

Restart Claude Code, then:

/teach-me any topic you want to understand

Some topics it works well on:

  • /teach-me <git commit SHA> — understand what a change actually did and why
  • /teach-me event-driven architecture — concepts, not just definitions
  • /teach-me this PR — onboard yourself to someone else's design decisions
  • /teach-me OAuth2 authorization code flow — the kind of thing you look up every six months
  • /teach-me why we deprecated the old middleware — organizational context, not just code

Two modes are available. Full mode generates HTML lesson files per phase (the experience described in this post). Simple mode stays entirely in chat — useful when you want a quick review without opening a browser.

No dependencies for teach-me. Unlike some skills in the repo that require MCP servers or external tools, teach-me works with a vanilla Claude Code installation. Clone, symlink, restart.

What I learned building it

The most interesting part wasn't the skill itself — it was running a teach-me session on the skill's own construction as a live test. Watching Claude enforce the gates on content I'd written was both gratifying and slightly humbling (I failed the Phase 3 quiz twice, as documented above).

A few things I'd do differently next time:

The highlighted-section approach for reteaching was the wrong abstraction. My first version regenerated the HTML file after each failed quiz, adding yellow highlights to the sections you missed. This cost ~2000 tokens per regeneration, added latency, and was solving the wrong problem. The real issue is understanding, not visibility. Reteaching in chat is cheaper, faster, and forces Claude to synthesize rather than just redisplay.

Layout bugs in templates are invisible until you actually use the template. I shipped the template with the quiz outside the lesson column, so the quiz was wider than the content. Classic "looks fine in isolation" bug. Running a real session caught it immediately.

The 3-attempt cap is important. Without it, a badly-written quiz question can trap a learner indefinitely. The cap forces a human judgment call — either slow down and restructure, or acknowledge this concept needs more time and come back to it. Both are valid outcomes.

The skill is available now. The repo has 8 skills total — interactive playgrounds, brainstorming workflows, a goal-writer, voice interaction. But teach-me is the one I find myself reaching for most.

If you try it, I'd love to hear what topics you run it on. Leave a comment below or find me on SAP Community.

GitHub: github.com/Dimi82/claude-playground-explorer — No dependencies, install with a single symlink.

entry.title: How to change the order of story filters in story reporting?
entry.content:
For the purpose of this exercise, I will move the Person ID filter from the last position to the first position:

 

To do this, proceed as follows:

- In Story Edit Mode, go to Settings → View Time Settings → Filter Panel.

- Change the Arrange Filters from Default to Custom Order

- Move the story filter to the desired position and save your changes by selecting Done.

The order of the story filters has now been changed.
